zotero.abstractNote: Compositional generalization—the ability to combine known concepts in novel ways—is a hallmark of human intelligence. While large language models (LLMs) have shown impressive performance on various tasks, their ability to extrapolate rules to out-of-distribution (OOD) prompts remains a subject of debate. In this work, we systematically investigate the compositional generalization capabilities of LLMs by introducing a novel benchmark, RuleExtrap, which requires models to extrapolate simple, rule-based transformations to unseen combinations of inputs. We find that while LLMs can perform well on in-distribution prompts, their performance drops significantly on OOD prompts that require rule extrapolation. Our analysis reveals that this failure is not due to a lack of knowledge, but rather a difficulty in applying rules compositionally. We further show that simple prompting strategies, such as chain-of-thought, do not fully mitigate this issue, suggesting that current LLMs struggle with the fundamental mechanism of rule extrapolation.
zotero.proceedingsTitle: Advances in Neural Information Processing Systems
